title: Entanglement-Enhanced Information Dynamics in Triple-Coin Discrete-Time Quantum Walks
abstract: This work investigates a discrete-time quantum walk on a one-dimensional lattice driven by three entangled coins, each initialized via a Hadamard operator. The walker moves only when all three coins yield identical outcomes (HHH or TTT), coupling an 8-dimensional coin Hilbert space to the position degree of freedom. We analyze fully separable, fully entangled, and intermediate initial coin states, computing the von Neumann entropy of reduced subsystems to derive the mutual information between coin and position over successive steps. Our results demonstrate that initial tripartite entanglement significantly accelerates the growth of mutual information and enhances coin-position correlations compared to separable initial conditions. Notably, GHZ-type entangled states exhibit non-monotonic short-time dynamics due to interference, yet ultimately yield up to 18% higher mutual information by the tenth step. These findings underscore the role of pre-walk entanglement as a resource for controlling information flow and spatial spreading in quantum-walk-based protocols, with implications for quantum transport, state transfer, and correlation engineering.
summary: This paper explores how using GHZ-entangled coins in a quantum walk can enhance the correlation between the coin and position degrees of freedom. While entanglement initially causes a delay due to destructive interference, it ultimately leads to significantly higher mutual information and faster spatial spreading. These findings suggest that pre-existing entanglement can be a powerful tool for engineering information dynamics in quantum protocols.
main_problem: Investigating how initial tripartite entanglement in a three-coin system affects the dynamics of mutual information and spatial spreading in a discrete-time quantum walk.
main_result: GHZ-type entangled states exhibit a 'delay-and-rise' non-monotonic dynamic that eventually leads to up to 18% higher mutual information between coin and position by the tenth step compared to separable states.
method: Analytical calculations for early-time interference mechanisms and numerical simulations of the quantum walk evolution on a 1D lattice.
model_or_system: A discrete-time quantum walk on a 1D lattice with an 8-dimensional coin Hilbert space (three qubits) and a 'unanimous-coin' displacement rule where movement only occurs if all three coins are in the same state.
key_observables: Quantum mutual information between coin and position, von Neumann entropy of reduced subsystems, and position entropy (spatial spreading).
important_parameters: Initial entanglement type (separable, bipartite, or GHZ), number of walk steps, and the dimensionality of the coin space.
main_assumptions: The global state remains pure (no decoherence or environmental coupling) and the position space is truncated to a finite lattice.
figures_summary: The notes refer to a Table 1 showing the evolution of mutual information for different initial states and a Figure 1 (description incomplete in notes) likely illustrating the dynamics of the walk.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the triple-coin model and displacement rule, compares different initial entanglement configurations, provides analytical derivations for short-time interference, presents numerical results for mutual information and spatial spreading, and discusses implications for quantum protocols.
why_interesting: It demonstrates how multipartite entanglement can be used as a resource to control information flow and accelerate correlations, which is highly relevant for designing efficient quantum state transfer and quantum sensing protocols.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

Spin-momentum entanglement.note: The paper directly studies the entanglement between the internal coin degrees of freedom and the external position/momentum degrees of freedom in a quantum walk.
